introCards.title: What is Bayquniyyah?
introCards.body: Bayquniyyah was written by Imam Umar ibn Muhammad al-Bayquni as a beginner poem in Mustalah al-Hadith. Students traditionally memorise it before larger works because it introduces terms such as sahih, hasan, da'if, mursal, mu'allaq, mudallas, shadh, munkar, mawdu', and others. Studying it builds a foundation and makes later hadith study easier.

introCards.title: How to Study Bayquniyyah
introCards.body: Read the Arabic line first, understand the simple meaning, then memorise gradually. Revise daily and test yourself with short questions. As you study hadith, use these terms to recognise how scholars describe reports, chains, narrators, and defects.

lessons.title: Opening and Purpose
lessons.arabic: أَبْدَأُ بِالْحَمْدِ مُصَلِّيًا عَلَى مُحَمَّدٍ خَيْرِ نَبِيٍّ أُرْسِلَا
lessons.english: Begin I do with praise, and prayers and salutations, on Muhammad the Noble Prophet, the finest ever commissioned.
lessons.explanation: The poem begins with praise of Allah and prayers upon the Prophet Muhammad, peace be upon him. This reminds learners that Islamic knowledge begins with humility, gratitude, and love for the Messenger whose hadith are being studied.

quiz.question: How does the poem begin?
options: With praise and prayers upon the Prophet

lessons.title: Hadith Divisions
lessons.arabic: وَذِي مِنْ أَقْسَامِ الْحَدِيثِ عِدَّهْ وَكُلُّ وَاحِدٍ أَتَى وَحَدَّهْ
lessons.english: So what follows are a number of the hadith divisions, and all of them do come with their definitions.
lessons.explanation: Bayquniyyah is organised around categories of hadith. Each term is introduced with a short definition. Beginners should treat these as first steps toward understanding how hadith scholars describe reports.

lessons.title: Sahih Hadith
lessons.arabic: أَوَّلُهَا الصَّحِيحُ وَهْوَ مَا اتَّصَلْ إِسْنَادُهُ وَلَمْ يَشُذَّ أَوْ يُعَلْ
lessons.english: The first of them is Sound: its chain is connected, and it does not contradict or have a hidden failing.
lessons.explanation: A sahih hadith has a connected chain and is free from serious contradiction and hidden defects. This is a beginner definition; actual grading requires qualified scholarship and detailed study.

quiz.question: Which term means a sound hadith?

lessons.title: Reliable Transmission
lessons.arabic: يَرْوِيهِ عَدْلٌ ضَابِطٌ عَنْ مِثْلِهِ مُعْتَمَدٌ فِي ضَبْطِهِ وَنَقْلِهِ
lessons.english: One upright and precise narrates from his like, reliable in precision as well as transmission.
lessons.explanation: The poem continues the definition of sahih. It points to upright narrators and accurate preservation. These are technical areas, so beginners should learn the vocabulary without attempting independent narrator judgement.

lessons.title: Hasan Hadith
lessons.arabic: وَالْحَسَنُ الْمَعْرُوفُ طُرْقًا وَغَدَتْ رِجَالُهُ لَا كَالصَّحِيحِ اشْتَهَرَتْ
lessons.english: The tradition that is Fair is one whose routes are known; its narrators are not as famed as those of the Sound.
lessons.explanation: Hasan is often translated as fair or good. It is below sahih in strength but still recognised in hadith terminology. This line helps learners distinguish basic authenticity terms.

quiz.question: Which term is commonly translated as fair or good?

lessons.title: Da'if Hadith
lessons.arabic: وَكُلُّ مَا عَنْ رُتْبَةِ الْحُسْنِ قَصُرْ فَهْوَ الضَّعِيفُ وَهْوَ أَقْسَامٌ كُثُرْ
lessons.english: All that falls below the rank of the Fair tradition is Weak, with many subdivisions.
lessons.explanation: Da'if means weak. Weakness has many types and levels. Beginners should avoid using weak reports to establish specific beliefs or practices unless guided by qualified scholarship.

lessons.title: Marfu' and Maqtu'
lessons.arabic: وَمَا أُضِيفَ لِلنَّبِيِّ الْمَرْفُوعُ وَمَا لِتَابِعٍ هُوَ الْمَقْطُوعُ
lessons.english: What is attributed to the Prophet is Raised; what belongs to a Successor is Maqtu'.
lessons.explanation: Marfu' refers to a report attributed to the Prophet, peace be upon him. Maqtu' refers to a report from a Successor. This is about attribution, not automatically authenticity.

quiz.question: A report attributed to the Prophet is called:

lessons.title: Musnad
lessons.arabic: وَالْمُسْنَدُ الْمُتَّصِلُ الْإِسْنَادِ مِنْ رَاوِيهِ حَتَّى الْمُصْطَفَى وَلَمْ يَبِنْ
lessons.english: The Musnad has a connected chain from its narrator to the Chosen One, without a break.
lessons.explanation: Musnad here describes a report with a connected chain reaching the Prophet, peace be upon him. It teaches learners to notice whether a chain is continuous.

quiz.question: Musnad in this line points to:
options: A connected chain to the Prophet

lessons.title: Muttasil
lessons.arabic: وَمَا بِسَمْعِ كُلِّ رَاوٍ يَتَّصِلْ إِسْنَادُهُ لِلْمُصْطَفَى فَالْمُتَّصِلْ
lessons.english: What is connected by the hearing of every narrator through to the Chosen One is Connected.
lessons.explanation: Muttasil means connected. Each narrator received the report from the one before him. This is one of the first chain concepts beginners should learn.

lessons.title: Musalsal
lessons.arabic: مُسَلْسَلٌ قُلْ مَا عَلَى وَصْفٍ أَتَى مِثْلُ أَمَا وَاللَّهِ أَنْبَأَنِي الْفَتَى
lessons.english: Linked is the tradition that comes with a repeated description, such as: By Allah, the lad informed me.
lessons.explanation: Musalsal reports have a repeated feature in the chain or wording, such as narrators using the same phrase or action. It is a special transmission pattern.

lessons.title: More Musalsal Examples
lessons.arabic: كَذَاكَ قَدْ حَدَّثَنِيهِ قَائِمَا أَوْ بَعْدَ أَنْ حَدَّثَنِي تَبَسَّمَا
lessons.english: Likewise: He told me while standing, or after he told me, he smiled.
lessons.explanation: This line gives examples of repeated descriptions in musalsal transmission. The repeated action or wording becomes part of how the report is identified.

quiz.question: This line gives examples of which term?

lessons.title: Aziz and Mashhur
lessons.arabic: عَزِيزٌ مَرْوِي اثْنَيْنِ أَوْ ثَلَاثَهْ مَشْهُورٌ مَرْوِي فَوْقَ مَا ثَلَاثَهْ
lessons.english: Aziz is related by two or three; Mashhur is related by more than three.
lessons.explanation: These terms relate to the number of transmitters. Aziz has a small number, while mashhur is more widely narrated. This is about spread, not automatically authenticity.

lessons.title: Mu'an'an and Mubham
lessons.arabic: مُعَنْعَنٌ كَعَنْ سَعِيدٍ عَنْ كَرَمْ وَمُبْهَمٌ مَا فِيهِ رَاوٍ لَمْ يُسَمْ
lessons.english: Mu'an'an is like 'from Sa'id from Karam'; Mubham contains a narrator who has not been named.
lessons.explanation: Mu'an'an uses wording such as 'from'. Mubham means an unnamed person appears in the chain. Both terms help learners pay attention to chain wording.

lessons.title: Ali and Nazil Chains
lessons.arabic: وَكُلُّ مَا قَلَّتْ رِجَالُهُ عَلاَ وَضِدُّهُ ذَاكَ الَّذِي قَدْ نَزَلاَ
lessons.english: Those whose men are few are Uplifted; the opposite are Descended.
lessons.explanation: A higher chain has fewer narrators between the collector and the source. A lower chain has more narrators. This is a chain-length concept.

quiz.question: A chain with fewer narrators is called:

lessons.title: Mawquf
lessons.arabic: وَمَا أَضَفْتَهُ إِلَى الأَصْحَابِ مِنْ قَوْلٍ وَفِعْلٍ فَهْوَ مَوْقُوفٌ زُكِنْ
lessons.english: What you attribute to the Companions from speech or action is known as Mawquf.
lessons.explanation: Mawquf refers to reports stopped at a Companion. It is not the same as a statement directly attributed to the Prophet, peace be upon him.

lessons.title: Mursal and Gharib
lessons.arabic: وَمُرْسَلٌ مِنْهُ الصَّحَابِيُّ سَقَطْ وَقُلْ غَرِيبٌ مَا رَوَى رَاوٍ فَقَطْ
lessons.english: Mursal is where the Companion is omitted; Gharib is what only one narrator related.
lessons.explanation: Mursal has a missing Companion link in this beginner definition. Gharib refers to a report narrated by one narrator at a point in the chain.

quiz.question: Gharib relates to:
options: A single narrator at a point

lessons.title: Munqati'
lessons.arabic: وَكُلُّ مَا لَمْ يَتَّصِلْ بِحَالِ إِسْنَادُهُ مُنْقَطِعُ الأَوْصَالِ
lessons.english: Every report whose chain is not connected is severed in its links.
lessons.explanation: Munqati' means disconnected. A break somewhere in the chain prevents the isnad from being fully connected.

lessons.title: Mu'dal and Mudallas
lessons.arabic: وَالْمُعْضَلُ السَّاقِطُ مِنْهُ اثْنَانِ وَمَا أَتَى مُدَلَّسًا نَوْعَانِ
lessons.english: Mu'dal is the one from which two are fallen; what comes as Mudallas has two types.
lessons.explanation: Mu'dal has two missing narrators in sequence. Mudallas involves concealment in transmission. These are more advanced chain-defect terms.

lessons.title: First Type of Tadlis
lessons.arabic: الأَوَّلُ الإِسْقَاطُ لِلشَّيْخِ وَأَنْ يَنْقُلَ عَمَّنْ فَوْقَهُ بِعَنْ وَأَنْ
lessons.english: The first is dropping the teacher and narrating from one above him using terms like 'from' and 'that'.
lessons.explanation: This line describes a form of tadlis where a narrator conceals the immediate teacher. Beginners should learn the idea, not use it to judge narrators independently.

quiz.question: This type of tadlis may hide:

lessons.title: Second Type of Tadlis
lessons.arabic: وَالثَّانِ لاَ يُسْقِطُهُ لَكِنْ يَصِفْ أَوْصَافَهُ بِمَا بِهِ لاَ يَنْعَرِفْ
lessons.english: The second does not drop him, but describes him with traits by which he is not recognised.
lessons.explanation: Another form of tadlis is describing a teacher in an unfamiliar way. The teacher is present, but the wording may make identification difficult.

lessons.title: Shadh and Maqlub
lessons.arabic: وَمَا يُخَالِفْ ثِقَةٌ بِهِ الْمَلَا فَالشَّاذُ وَالْمَقْلُوبُ قِسْمَانِ تَلَا
lessons.english: What a trusted narrator reports in contradiction to the greater number is Shadh; Maqlub has two kinds.
lessons.explanation: Shadh involves a contradiction by a reliable narrator against stronger or more numerous narrations. Maqlub means something has been switched.

lessons.title: Types of Maqlub
lessons.arabic: إِبْدَالُ رَاوٍ مَا بِرَاوٍ قِسْمُ وَقَلْبُ إِسْنَادٍ لِمَتْنٍ قِسْمُ
lessons.english: One type is switching one narrator for another; another is attaching a chain to a different text.
lessons.explanation: Maqlub means switched or reversed. It may involve a narrator being swapped or a chain being attached to the wrong matn.

lessons.title: Fard
lessons.arabic: وَالْفَرْدُ مَا قَيَّدْتَهُ بِثِقَةٍ أَوْ جَمْعٍ أَوْ قَصْرٍ عَلَى رِوَايَةٍ
lessons.english: Fard is what you restrict to a trustworthy person, a group, or a region by way of narration.
lessons.explanation: Fard refers to singularity or restriction in transmission. It may be singular through a narrator, group, place, or route.

lessons.title: Mu'allal
lessons.arabic: وَمَا بِعِلَّةٍ غُمُوضٍ أَوْ خَفَا مُعَلَّلٌ عِنْدَهُمْ قَدْ عُرِفَا
lessons.english: Whatever contains a subtle or hidden flaw is known among them as Mu'allal.
lessons.explanation: Mu'allal refers to a report with a hidden defect. This is one of the most delicate areas of hadith criticism and requires expert scholarship.

lessons.title: Mudtarib
lessons.arabic: وَذُو اخْتِلَافِ سَنَدٍ أَوْ مَتْنِ مُضْطَرِبٌ عِنْدَ أُهَيْلِ الْفَنِّ
lessons.english: Those that contain variance in their text or chain are called Mudtarib by the masters of this field.
lessons.explanation: Mudtarib refers to conflicting forms of a report where the differences cannot be reconciled or preferred. It can occur in the chain or text.

lessons.title: Mudraj
lessons.arabic: وَالْمُدْرَجَاتُ فِي الْحَدِيثِ مَا أَتَتْ مِنْ بَعْضِ أَلْفَاظِ الرُّوَاةِ اتَّصَلَتْ
lessons.english: Interjections in hadith are words that came from some narrators and became connected to the report.
lessons.explanation: Mudraj means inserted wording. A narrator's words may appear within a report, so scholars identify what belongs to the hadith and what is inserted.

lessons.title: Mudabbaj
lessons.arabic: وَمَا رَوَى كُلُّ قَرِينٍ عَنْ أَخِيهِ مُدَبَّجٌ فَاعْرِفْهُ حَقًّا وَانْتَخِهِ
lessons.english: When each peer narrates from his companion, it is Mudabbaj, so know it well.
lessons.explanation: Mudabbaj occurs when peers narrate from one another. It is a relationship term in transmission, showing how narrators may narrate mutually.

lessons.title: Muttafiq and Muftariq
lessons.arabic: مُتَّفِقٌ لَفْظًا وَخَطًّا مُتَّفِقْ وَضِدُّهُ فِيمَا ذَكَرْنَا الْمُفْتَرِقْ
lessons.english: Agreeing in wording and script is Muttafiq; its opposite is Muftariq.
lessons.explanation: These terms relate to names that may look or sound alike. They help scholars distinguish narrators and avoid confusing identities.

lessons.title: Mu'talif and Mukhtalif
lessons.arabic: مُؤْتَلِفٌ مُتَّفِقُ الْخَطِّ فَقَطْ وَضِدُّهُ مُخْتَلِفٌ فَاخْشَ الْغَلَطْ
lessons.english: Mu'talif agrees only in script; its opposite is Mukhtalif, so beware of mistakes.
lessons.explanation: This line warns about names that may be written similarly but pronounced or identified differently. Careful reading protects against narrator confusion.

quiz.question: The warning here is to avoid:

lessons.title: Munkar
lessons.arabic: وَالْمُنْكَرُ الْفَرْدُ بِهِ رَاوٍ غَدَا تَعْدِيلُهُ لاَ يَحْمِلُ التَّفَرُّدَا
lessons.english: Munkar is the solitary report of a narrator whose reliability cannot bear that singularity.
lessons.explanation: Munkar is a criticism term connected to a problematic solitary report. Beginners should recognise the word but not use it to grade reports independently.

lessons.title: Matruk
lessons.arabic: مَتْرُوكُهُ مَا وَاحِدٌ بِهِ انْفَرَدْ وَأَجْمَعُوا لِضَعْفِهِ فَهُوَ كَرَدْ
lessons.english: Matruk is what one person alone related, while scholars agreed on his weakness, so it is rejected.
lessons.explanation: Matruk means abandoned or discarded. It is a serious weakness term. This should be understood as terminology, not applied without scholarship.

lessons.title: Mawdu'
lessons.arabic: وَالْكَذِبُ الْمُخْتَلَقُ الْمَصْنُوعُ عَلَى النَّبِيِّ فَذَلِكَ الْمَوْضُوعُ
lessons.english: The lie, concocted and contrived upon the Prophet, is Fabricated.
lessons.explanation: Mawdu' means fabricated. It refers to a report falsely attributed to the Prophet, peace be upon him. This is a severe term and must be handled carefully.

lessons.title: Naming the Poem
lessons.arabic: وَقَدْ أَتَتْ كَالْجَوْهَرِ الْمَكْنُونِ سَمَّيْتُهَا مَنْظُومَةَ الْبَيْقُونِي
lessons.english: It has come like a hidden pearl; I named it the Ode of Bayquni.
lessons.explanation: The author names the poem and presents it as a concise collection of terms. Its short format made it easy for beginners to memorise and review.

lessons.title: Completion of the Poem
lessons.arabic: فَوْقَ الثَّلاَثِينَ بِأَرْبَعٍ أَتَتْ أَقْسَامُهَا تَمَّتْ بِخَيْرٍ خُتِمَتْ
lessons.english: By four beyond thirty the divisions have come; their lines are complete and ended with good.
lessons.explanation: The poem closes by noting that its categories are thirty-four. This gives learners a complete beginner map of key mustalah al-hadith terms.

quiz.question: How many divisions does the poem point to?
